data.title: ICE Canola Up at Midday
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--The ICE Futures canola market was stronger at midday Friday, finding spillover support from advances in Chicago soybeans and soyoil.

Optimism over the possibility of easing tensions with China was also supportive. A trade delegation to the major canola buying country had "constructive discussions" with Chinese officials, according to a statement from Prime Minister Mark Carney's office.

"The visit paves the way for further constructive engagement with Chinese counterparts to find pragmatic solutions to shared trade concerns," the statement added.

While tight old crop supplies also underpinned the futures, the advancing new crop harvest tempered the gains.

The U.S. Department of Agriculture will release updated supply/demand estimates at 11:00 a.m. CDT, with any surprises in the data likely to set the tone of trade ahead of the close.

An estimated 25,700 canola contracts traded as of 10:17 a.m. CDT. 




  Prices in Canadian dollars per metric ton at 10:17 a.m. CDT: 


 
           Price      Change 
Nov       638.40     up 6.70 
Jan       650.50     up 6.40 
Mar       661.80     up 6.30 
May       671.80     up 6.20 
 



  Source: Commodity News Service Canada, news@marketsfarm.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  September 12, 2025 11:45 ET (15:45 GMT)

data.title: AM Best Affirms Credit Ratings of Horace Mann Educators Corporation and Its Subsidiaries
data.description:
AM Best Affirms Credit Ratings of Horace Mann Educators Corporation and Its Subsidiaries

AM Best has affirmed the Financial Strength Rating (FSR) of A (Excellent) and the Long-Term Issuer Credit Ratings (Long-Term ICR) of “a” (Excellent) of the property/casualty (P/C) and life/health (L/H) members of Horace Mann Educators Corporation (HMEC) (headquartered in Springfield, IL) [NYSE: HMN]. Additionally, AM Best has affirmed the Long-Term ICR of “bbb” (Good) of HMEC and the associated Long-Term Issue Credit Ratings (Long-Term IRs). The outlook of these Credit Ratings (ratings) is stable. (See below for a detailed list of the subsidiaries and Long-Term IRs.)

These ratings reflect HMEC’s balance sheet strength, which AM Best assesses as very strong, as well as its adequate operating performance, neutral business profile and appropriate enterprise risk management (ERM).

HMEC’s Long-Term ICR is driven by the group’s very strong balance sheet strength assessment, which is supported by its strongest risk-adjusted capitalization, as measured by Best’s Capital Adequacy Ratio (BCAR), and utilization of highly rated reinsurers with a strong level of reinsurance catastrophe protection. The group’s capital position reflects its favorable liquidity posture and overall financial flexibility with access to capital markets with financial leverage and interest coverage metrics that are at levels acceptable for its current rating. However, these positive factors are offset partially by restricted fungibility of capital as excess capital rests within the operating entities and a higher allocation to less liquid asset classes, and structured securities, along with lower-rated investment-grade bonds.

HMEC benefits from a well-diversified operating profile within P/C, life and supplemental benefit business segments. Overall results have been positive over the recent five-year period, although the group has experienced volatility in its P/C operations, albeit improving recently. The P/C segment’s results have struggled in years past as it was impacted by severe weather events, as well as rising loss costs, largely driven by inflation in the auto and property lines of business. Strong net investment income helped offset losses in recent years and continues to boost overall earnings. Significant rate and non-rate underwriting actions within the auto and property lines have contributed to significant improvements in overall operating results. Life and supplemental benefit results have been favorable over the three-year period and remain stable.

HMEC continues to maintain a strong market position within its niche through its name brand recognition within the educator’s market and policyholder loyalty. These advantages provide cross marketing opportunities between P/C product lines, as well as life, annuity and supplemental products. The group operates in target market competing with a number of national providers of auto, home, and life. The group’s business is diversified on a product level and a geographic level as the multitude of products offered showcase moderate diversification, while the top five states make up less than 40% of premiums.

The group operates under a formal ERM framework, which is appropriate for the scope and complexity of its risk profile. Within HMEC, ERM is an ongoing assessment process used to identify, manage and/or mitigate risk, which continues to influence the company’s strategy and direction. The group employs an extensive reinsurance program with high quality reinsurers. Reinsurance is purchased at the operating entity level and tailored to its specific business segment.

The FSR of A (Excellent) and the Long-Term ICRs of “a” (Excellent) have been affirmed with stable outlooks for the following P/C and L/H subsidiaries of Horace Mann Educators Corporation:

  • Horace Mann Insurance Company
  • Horace Mann Property & Casualty Insurance Company
  • Horace Mann Life Insurance Company
  • Teachers Insurance Company
  • National Teachers Associates Life Insurance Company
  • Madison National Life Insurance Company, Inc.
  • NTA Life Insurance Company of New York

The following Long-Term IRs have been affirmed with stable outlooks:

Horace Mann Educators Corporation—
-- “bbb” (Good) on $250 million 4.5% senior unsecured notes, due 2025
-- “bbb” (Good) on $300 million 7.25 % senior unsecured notes, due 2028

This press release relates to Credit Ratings that have been published on AM Best’s website. For all rating information relating to the release and pertinent disclosures, including details of the office responsible for issuing each of the individual ratings referenced in this release, please see AM Best’s Recent Rating Activity web page. For additional information regarding the use and limitations of Credit Rating opinions, please view Guide to Best's Credit Ratings. For information on the proper use of Best’s Credit Ratings, Best’s Performance Assessments, Best’s Preliminary Credit Assessments and AM Best press releases, please view Guide to Proper Use of Best’s Ratings & Assessments.

AM Best is a global credit rating agency, news publisher and data analytics provider specializing in the insurance industry. Headquartered in the United States, the company does business in over 100 countries with regional offices in London, Amsterdam, Dubai, Hong Kong, Singapore and Mexico City. For more information, visit www.ambest.com.

Copyright © 2025 by A.M. Best Rating Services, Inc. and/or its affiliates. ALL RIGHTS RESERVED.

data.title: Luma AI and the Clio Awards Partner to Launch Landmark AI Creative Challenge, Bringing Dream Machine to the Next Generation of Talent
data.description:
Luma AI and the Clio Awards Partner to Launch Landmark AI Creative Challenge, Bringing Dream Machine to the Next Generation of Talent

Submissions now open for junior to mid-level creatives and recent graduates to build 10-second vertical ads with Luma’s Dream Machine; three finalists will be recognized at the Clio Creative Summit in New York City on October 20

Luma AI, one of the world's most advanced generative AI companies, and The Clio Awards, the global awards program celebrating the creative industry for nearly 70 years, today announced a landmark partnership to launch the “AI Challenge – Powered by Dream Machine.” This creative brief is designed to inspire and empower the next wave of creative professionals by putting cutting-edge AI video tools directly into their hands and giving them an opportunity to share their ideas on The Clio Awards stage.

This press release features multimedia. View the full release here: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20250911995617/en/

Luma AI and the Clio Awards Partner to Launch Landmark AI Creative Challenge, Bringing Dream Machine to the Next Generation of Talent

At the center of the challenge is Dream Machine, Luma AI’s breakthrough platform that enables anyone to produce production-ready video instantly from text, image, or video prompts. Dream Machine makes production-ready video more accessible, enabling teams to stretch creative budgets further and explore more concepts within existing resources. The platform’s capabilities, from rapid A/B testing to seamless iteration of visual styles and storytelling approaches, are helping advertisers and creators rethink what’s possible in content creation.

By teaming up with The Clio Awards, Luma AI is bringing these capabilities to a broader creative community, reinforcing both organizations’ commitment to innovation. The Clio Awards have long celebrated the work that pushes the industry forward, and this collaboration showcases how AI can enhance creativity.

Caroline Ingeborn, Chief Operating Officer of Luma AI, said, “This AI Challenge is about giving the next generation of talent the tools to imagine without limits. Dream Machine was built to empower creators, and this partnership with The Clio Awards opens the door for emerging voices to experiment, innovate, and show the industry what’s possible when AI and creativity come together.”

The challenge invites junior to mid-level creatives and recent graduates to submit 10-second vertical ads built with Dream Machine. Entries must demonstrate storytelling power, brand integration, and the unique visual effects that only AI can provide. Finalists will receive six months of Dream Machine Unlimited access, recognition at the Clio Creative Summit in New York City on October 20, 2025, and the chance to join a live roundtable discussion with Luma executives.

Submissions are open now through October 6 and are free for the Clio community. Participants will also gain access to the Luma AI Dream Lab for best practice sessions and technical support to guide their creative process.

For more information and to submit entries, visit: https://info.clios.com/specialty-lumaai

About Luma AI

Luma AI is building a multimodal general intelligence that can understand, generate, and operate in the physical world. Its flagship platform, Dream Machine, enables creatives everywhere to generate professional-grade video and images, and now powers creation for over 30 million users. In 2025, Luma released Ray2, a frontier video generative model capable of creating realistic visuals with stunning detail and natural motion. Luma’s models are utilized by top entertainment studios, ad agencies, and technology leaders, including Adobe and AWS, and are available via subscription or API. The company is backed by Andreessen Horowitz, AWS, Amplify Partners, and Matrix Partners.

About The Clio Awards

The Clio Awards were founded in 1959 to honor excellence in advertising around the world. The prestigious program, which has continuously evolved alongside the creative industry it champions, today celebrates boundary-pushing marketing, advertising and communications from the brands, agencies and organizations that shape popular culture and drive global commerce. Each cycle, The Clio Awards brings together leading creative talent for an immersive judging experience to determine which entries will earn the prestigious Grand, Gold, Silver, and Bronze Clio statues.

Peter Binazeski
Head of Communications
Luma AI
peter@lumalabs.ai

View source version on businesswire.com: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20250911995617/en/

data.title: Microsoft Settles EU Antitrust Probe Over Teams After Officials Accept Concessions — 3rd Update
data.description:
By Mauro Orru and Edith Hancock 




  The European Union accepted proposals from Microsoft over how it distributes its Teams collaboration platform in the bloc, settling an antitrust probe that could have resulted in a fine for the tech giant. 



  The European Commission, the EU's executive arm, opened a formal investigation in 2023 into Microsoft's bundling of Teams with its popular Office productivity software after receiving a complaint from business-messaging-app maker Slack Technologies, now owned by Salesforce. 



  Officials in the bloc raised preliminary concerns that Microsoft broke antitrust rules, arguing that tying Teams to popular applications like Word and Outlook included in its Office 365 and Microsoft 365 suites gave Teams an unfair advantage. 



  After the EU launched its investigation, Microsoft made changes to how it distributed Teams, offering business customers versions of its productivity suites without Teams. However, the commission said those changes didn't go far enough. 



  Microsoft subsequently proposed a series of commitments that the EU has now accepted. These include making versions of Office 365 and Microsoft 365 suites available without Teams at a reduced price and allowing customers with long-term licenses to switch to suites without Teams. 



  Microsoft will also ensure there is interoperability for key functionalities between tools that compete with Teams and certain Microsoft products. In addition, the company will let customers move their data out of Teams, changes the EU said would open up the market for other providers of communication and collaboration tools in the bloc. 



  The commitments will remain in force for seven years, the bloc said, with those related to interoperability and data portability lasting 10 years. A trustee will monitor their implementation and act as mediator between Microsoft and third parties in case of disputes. 



  "We appreciate the dialog with the commission that led to this agreement, and we turn now to implementing these new obligations promptly and fully," Nanna-Louise Linde, Microsoft's vice president of European government affairs, said. 



  Sabastian Niles, president and chief legal officer at Slack owner Salesforce, welcomed the resolution, calling it a meaningful step forward. 



  "Microsoft's anticompetitive tying of Teams has harmed businesses, denied customers fair choice, and resulted in many years of lost competition," Niles said. "We look forward to the commission's rigorous monitoring and enforcement of the commitments to ensure Microsoft fully complies with its obligations." 



  The settlement is a win for Microsoft as the company is now able to put the case behind it and avoid a fine that could have irked President Trump at a delicate time for trade relations. 



  Last week, the EU fined Alphabet's Google nearly $3.5 billion for abusing the dominance of its advertising-technology tools. Google said it would appeal the EU decision and fine, which it described as wrong and unjustified. 



  Trump called the move discriminatory and threatened to open a Section 301 trade investigation "to nullify the unfair penalties" that he said are being charged to American companies. 



  Speaking at a conference in Florence on Friday, Teresa Ribera, the EU's top competition enforcer, said the bloc wouldn't shy away from antitrust enforcement. However, she also stressed the importance of soft enforcement, when officials engage in dialog with companies they suspect of breaches in a bid to get concessions, as happened in the Microsoft case. 



  "This decision shows that our soft enforcement approach can be particularly important in digital markets, where new products and integration strategies often challenge the boundaries of regulation. So, we need quick and cooperative interventions," she said. 





  Write to Mauro Orru at mauro.orru@wsj.com and Edith Hancock at edith.hancock@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  September 12, 2025 11:36 ET (15:36 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: SMBC Americas’ Christine Ferris Earns No.2 Spot on American Banker’s 2025 Most Powerful Women in Banking NEXT List
data.description:
SMBC Americas’ Christine Ferris Earns No.2 Spot on American Banker’s 2025 Most Powerful Women in Banking NEXT List

SMBC Americas is proud to announce that Christine Ferris, Head of Securitized Products, has earned the No.2 spot on American Banker’s 2025 Most Powerful Women in Banking NEXT list.

“Christine’s leadership, expertise and drive to achieving client success have been crucial to the strength of our growing securitized products business in the Americas,” said Scott Ashby, Deputy Head of the Americas Division and President of SMBC Nikko Securities America. “Beyond impeccable execution on behalf of clients, Christine has supported SMBC colleagues through mentorship opportunities and sets a standard of excellence across the bank.”

Throughout her career, Christine has proven herself an industry leader in finance. Christine and her team have driven SMBC’s CLO origination and syndication business while updating key infrastructure.

For more than 23 years, American Banker’s Most Powerful Women in Banking rankings have celebrated top-performing female senior executives based on their accomplishments and influence. To see the full NEXT 2025 list and honoree profiles, visit: https://www.americanbanker.com/list/the-most-powerful-women-in-banking-next-2025.

About SMBC
SMBC Group is a top-tier global financial group. Headquartered in Tokyo and with a 400-year history, SMBC Group offers a diverse range of financial services, including banking, leasing, securities, credit cards, and consumer finance. The Group has more than 150 offices and 120,000 employees worldwide in nearly 40 countries. Sumitomo Mitsui Financial Group, Inc. (SMFG) is the holding company of SMBC Group, which is one of the three largest banking groups in Japan. SMFG’s shares trade on the Tokyo, Nagoya, and ADRs on the New York (NYSE: SMFG) stock exchanges.

In the Americas, SMBC Group has a presence in the U.S., Canada, Mexico, Brazil, Chile, Colombia, and Peru. The Group’s operating companies in the Americas include Sumitomo Mitsui Banking Corp. (SMBC), SMBC Americas Holdings, Inc., SMBC Nikko Securities America, Inc., SMBC Nikko Securities Canada, Ltd., SMBC Capital Markets, Inc., SMBC MANUBANK, JRI America, Inc., SMBC Leasing and Finance, Inc., Banco Sumitomo Mitsui Brasileiro S.A., and Sumitomo Mitsui Finance and Leasing Co., Ltd. (collectively, SMBC Group Americas Division). For more information, please visit www.smbcgroup.com

MediaRelationsAmericas@SMBCGroup.com

data.title: Always picking up the tab or lending money you don't have? It could be 'financial fawning' - here's how to stop.
data.description:
By Genna Contino

'Fawning is a survival response often learned in childhood,' one psychologist says

A money-related offshoot to a common trauma response is "financial fawning," which can impact how we spend, talk about money in relationships or interact with co-workers.

You're out to dinner with friends. Everyone else ordered steak, but you only ordered an appetizer to save money. At the end of the meal, someone suggests splitting the check evenly. You're filled with dread, but you put down your credit card without saying a word - after all, you don't want to make a fuss.

This is just one example of how trying to please others can take a toll not just mentally but also financially, especially if it becomes a habit.

This behavior can sometimes stem from an involuntary reaction to stress called "fawning," according to psychologist Ingrid Clayton, who describes it in her new book as the "fourth F of trauma responses" - along with fighting, fleeing and freezing.

Fawners "mirror or merge with someone else's desires or expectations, to defuse conflict rather than confront it directly. Because it's their best chance to stay safe. At least for now," Clayton wrote in her book, "Fawning: Why the Need to Please Makes Us Lose Ourselves - and How to Find Our Way Back."

A money-related offshoot of fawning is "financial fawning," according to Chantel Chapman, the CEO and founder of an educational program called Trauma of Money that certifies professionals and organizations in trauma-informed approaches to money.

While plenty of anecdotal data illustrates financial fawning, Chapman said, the concept is still so new that formal research-gathering has just begun. But some broader financial survey data sheds light on how these behaviors appear in our day-to-day life.

See more: I'm single and gave my friend a wedding gift - but she didn't get me a housewarming present. Am I wrong to feel snubbed?

For example, about one in three people who say they have loaned money to a friend said they didn't get their money back, according to a recent LendingTree (TREE) report, which could represent the risk-taking habits that come with financial fawning. Respondents to a separate 2024 LendingTree survey reported overspending to "feel like a better friend/family member," "to get a promotion or raise at work" or "to reciprocate generosity."

"It's like you skip the cognitive part and you go right to survival," Chapman said. "If you've learned along the way that the best way to survive is to please, appease and caretake, it becomes a quick, urgent response."

Fawning is a common workplace survival mechanism

Fawning is a survival response often learned in childhood to navigate a threatening or dysfunctional environment in which someone found it easier to keep the peace rather than attempt to stand up for themselves and risk getting hurt. These behaviors can bleed into adulthood, causing people to instinctively appease others to de-escalate perceived threats, even if it's not in their best interest.

Because this is a deeply ingrained nervous-system response, Clayton said, it's difficult to simply set a boundary and get over it.

See more: Would getting a $20 bonus at work offend you? How company rewards can backfire.

For those whose trauma stems from an environment where they felt powerless, learned fawning behaviors can manifest in adulthood as an obsession with success and achievement, where financial security becomes a substitute for self-worth. This behavior often plays out in the workplace, where employees might find that fawning becomes a "survival mechanism" to keep their job or climb the corporate ladder, according to Rahkim Sabree, an expert in financial trauma.

This might look like an employee offering to work overtime despite feeling overwhelmed, or buying drinks they can't afford at a happy hour just to get face time with a boss.

"In the workplace, it's very clear that you cannot engage in a fight response, you cannot engage in a flight response, you cannot engage in a freeze response," Sabree said. "So fawning may feel like the only survival mechanism that you can engage in."

This is especially true for women and people of color, he said, who have historically earned less and held fewer positions of power. Code switching, where an employee might change their behavior or way of speaking in order to blend into different social settings, is another example of fawning in the workplace, Sabree said.

"It makes you as the individual stand out less," Sabree said. "It can be a survival response in order for you to maintain your employment."

From the archives (June 2025): When it's OK to burn a bridge at work - and how to do it with no regrets

Fawners' behavior in the workplace can look similar to how they approach romantic relationships, Chapman said. Someone who learned to keep the peace in a relationship might try to actively avoid engaging in the kind of aggressive behavior that was once directed at them.

"The person who is the fawner will be overgiving and they might be overpaying for things, gifting a lot of things," she said. "And then when it comes time to have hard conversations around money, it often is a challenge bringing that up. It creates a power dynamic in the relationship that is typically not very sustainable."

Overspending can be fawning

For others, a lack of self-worth might lead them to give money away in order to feel connected and valued. Fawners see others' needs as greater than their own, meaning they might regularly pick up the bar tab for their entire friend group or lend their partner money for "emergencies" that seem to occur weekly.

In her book, Clayton wrote about one client who inherited a nest egg from her father when he died, in addition to receiving money as part of a divorce settlement. The client felt undeserving of the money and thought people would treat her differently if they viewed her as "rich." Because she saw others' needs as greater than her own, she constantly found herself giving money away, even to people who didn't need it.

See more: How 'f- around and find out' parenting can make kids 'fantastic with money'

"It was like she was a sieve and [money] was just pouring out and she didn't really know why," Clayton said. "There wasn't a conscious choice to give it all away. That was certainly never her intention."

The cost of financial fawning isn't just financial - it can also be psychological, resulting in a loss of agency, damaged self-worth and feelings of shame. Fawners exhibit a pattern of self-abandonment in an attempt to earn validation and safety.

To be clear, not all instances of overspending or financial people pleasing are fawning, said Naomi Win, a behavioral finance analyst at Orion, a company that provides wealth technology services for financial advisers.

It's important to narrowly define financial fawning as a coping mechanism in response to a perceived threat or danger where someone acts against their own financial interest to survive the situation, said Win, who is also a licensed psychologist. Picking up a group's dinner bill might not be motivated by fawning, but rather by cultural values, religious beliefs or simple generosity.

See more: 5 mental tricks to reset your relationship with credit cards as Americans pile on debt

"I think it can be a little dangerous to refer to all of those things as fawning, since ... it's a word for trauma response," she said. "The reason I'm a bit hesitant around that is because applying therapeutic terms can really broadly flatten the intentions and motivations and be very reductionist, and it risks pathologizing presentations of financial behaviors."

How to recognize 'financial fawning' - and how to break the pattern

So how do you know if you're fawning? If giving a gift or paying for a meal feels good in the moment but later breeds resentment, Win said, that can signal that a boundary has been crossed and has led to the erosion of one's sense of control.

"You'll know when you've crossed the line between generosity to financial fawning when the act engenders resentment," she said.

From the archives (July 2025): How to keep your kid's Roblox or 'Fortnite' habit from wrecking your credit-card bill

For people who fawn in the workplace, Sabree advises taking stock of financial resources and marketable skills to create a strategic exit plan rather than letting resentment build to a boiling point and quitting on a whim. That way, the person can mitigate both the psychological and financial fallout from leaving a job.

For people who fawn in the form of overspending, Win recommends creating a budget that includes categories like "gift giving" or "treating friends." Setting expectations before going out to dinner, such as by choosing an affordable restaurant or suggesting separate checks ahead of time, can be a proactive move as well.

From the archives (June 2025): TikTok's latest budget hack is 'dating for dinner' - which says a lot about love and money right now

Breaking these patterns can be difficult but not impossible. By acknowledging that fawning is not a personal failing but instead is a response to past trauma or systemic disempowerment, Clayton said, people can move forward and stop feeling like they're in a constant battle for survival.

"We come by these patterns, these behaviors, honestly, and we don't have to live in a survival response 24/7," Clayton said. "We can have more freedom."

Have questions about the real-estate market? Post them here and MarketWatch's real-estate reporter, Aarthi Swaminathan, may answer them during a live Q&A on Friday, Sept. 12, from 1 p.m. to 2 p.m. Eastern time.

-Genna Contino

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  09-12-25 1124ET

data.title: U.K. Targets Russia With New Sanctions on Oil -2-
data.description:
London Miners Top FTSE 100 Index as Gold, Silver Prices Rise -- Market Talk

0727 GMT - Miners lead the gains on the FTSE 100 in opening trade. Silver and gold miner Fresnillo tops the index, with shares trading around 4% higher. Others such as Anglo American, Endeavour Mining and Glencore rise around 2%. The rise comes as gold trades up 0.5% at $3,653 a troy ounce, while silver rises 1.3% to $42.1 an ounce. Gold is being boosted by bets from investors that the U.S. Federal Reserve starts cutting rates, a weaker dollar and continuing geopolitical tensions, Swissquote Bank analyst Ipek Ozkardeskaya writes. Thursday's economic data from the U.S. has given the Federal Reserve the green light to cut rates next week, Ozkardeskaya adds. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

--

Gold Could Hit $3,800/Oz by Year End -- Market Talk

0642 GMT - UBS reckons gold will reach $3,800/oz by the end of 2025, up from a previous forecast of $3,500/oz. By mid-2026, the price could be around $3,900/oz, it reckons. The bank had previously projected $3,700/oz gold mid next year. UBS says expectations that the Federal Reserve will restart its easing cycle due to weak jobs data are fueling investor appetite. Ongoing geopolitical uncertainties are also underpinning demand, it says. UBS expects ETF holdings will rise near a previous record high of just above 3,900 metric tons by the end of 2025. UBS stays long gold in its global asset allocation, it says. Spot gold is up 0.5% at $3,651.49. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

--

Broadly Flat Commodities Complex Masks Diverging Markets -- Market Talk

0501 GMT - Aggregate global commodity prices are tracking--and will likely continue to track--broadly sideways, says HSBC economist Paul Bloxham. "However, this overall picture hides considerable divergence, volatility and market fragmentation below the surface," says Bloxham. He highlights record-high gold prices, volatile copper markets and softer oil prices, among other moves. "'Geoeconomics,' a nexus of geopolitics and economics, is increasingly driving commodity market dynamics" from gold and oil to soy beans and rare earths, says Bloxham. "More price volatility, shorter commodity price cycles and greater market fragmentation are the results," he says. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

--

Palm Oil Production Set to Peak in Sept-Oct -- Market Talk

0256 GMT - Palm oil production could peak again in September-October before tapering off, supported by favorable weather that should boost fruit formation, Apex Securities analyst Steven Chong says in a note. Export demand may remain subdued in September, as palm oil inventories in China and India were elevated in July due to aggressive restocking. Weaker soybean oil prices and rising supply may cap upside for CPO prices, he reckons. Apex Securities maintains a neutral rating on Malaysia's plantation sector, and pegs SD Guthrie as its top pick. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

--

Palm Oil Rises on Stronger Soybean Oil, Palm Olein -- Market Talk

0237 GMT - Palm oil futures rise in Asian trade, tracking stronger overnight soybean oil prices on the Chicago Board of Trade and higher palm olein prices on the Dalian Commodity Exchange, AmInvestment Bank says in a note. However, CPO prices may remain under pressure due to a rise in palm oil supply, it adds. AmInvestment Bank pegs CPO futures support at 4,377 ringgit a ton and resistance at 4,513 ringgit a ton. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives November contract is higher by 28 ringgit at 4,482 ringgit a ton. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

--

Nickel Gains Amid Supply Concerns -- Market Talk

0157 GMT - Nickel climbs in the Asian session amid supply concerns. Indonesia's forestry task force has taken over more than 148 hectares of a mine owned by Weda Bay Nickel due to violations of its forestry permit, say ANZ Research analysts in a note. While joint owner Eramet sees no impact on nickel mining operations at this stage, the analysts note that the seizure is Indonesia's first since President Prabowo Subianto pledged to crack down on illegal mining. The three-month LME contract for nickel is 1.1% higher at $15,315.00 a ton. (megan.cheah@wsj.com) 




  Write to Barcelona Editors at barcelonaeditors@dowjones.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  September 12, 2025 11:16 ET (15:16 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: U.K. Targets Russia With New Sanctions on Oil Fleet, Defense Suppliers — Commodities Roundup
data.description:
MARKET MOVEMENTS:

--Brent crude oil is up 1.6% to $67.43 a barrel

--European benchmark gas is up 0.9% to 32.62 euros a megawatt-hour

--Gold futures are up 0.3% to $3,686.50 a troy ounce

--LME three-month copper futures are up 0.2% to $10,075 a metric ton 




  TOP STORY: 



  U.K. Targets Russia With New Sanctions on Oil Fleet, Defense Suppliers 



  The U.K. outlined a fresh package of 100 sanctions against Russia that targets ships carrying Russian oil as well as companies supplying electronics, chemicals and explosives used to manufacture weapons. 



  "International action to increase economic pressure on Russia and to cut off critical cash flows which he desperately needs to pay for this illegal war is vital," said U.K. Foreign Secretary Yvette Cooper on Friday. 





  OTHER STORIES: 



  Canada on Course to Emerge as Energy Superpower, Resources Minister Says 



  OTTAWA--Canada is on the path to becoming an energy superpower, the country's resources minister said after his government backed the expansion of a liquefied natural-gas facility and signaled its willingness to support a new crude-oil pipeline. 



  Tim Hodgson said investors are noticing the government's shift on pursuing nation-building resource projects to help rewire an economy trying to reduce its reliance on a protectionist White House. He said senior officials are receiving pitches daily on projects that can help ship Canada's vast minerals and oil-and-gas reserves to non-U.S. markets. 



  -- 



  Vallourec Wins $1 Billion Contract From Petrobras 



  Vallourec said it won a major contract to supply Brazil's state-controlled oil company Petrobras with specialized steel pipes for oil and gas drilling and production. 



  The contract could generate total revenue of up to $1 billion over the 2026 to 2029 period, the French steel tube maker said Thursday. 





  MARKET TALKS: 



  Russia Sanctions Put Bid Back in Oil 



  1033 ET - Oil futures are recovering from the previous day's losses as the U.K. announces new sanctions on Russia and the U.S. urges Europe to put tariff pressure on buyers of Russian oil, namely India and China. "The Russia/Ukraine war remains a positive to crude prices with Ukraine hitting a major oil export facility in Russia late yesterday," Dennis Kissler of BOK Financial says in a note. "Russia exports nearly 7 million barrels a day, therefore any disruptions whether from tariffs or war conflicts could be a bullish catalyst for crude prices near-term." WTI is up 1.5% at $63.28 a barrel and Brent is up 1.6% at $67.43. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com) 



  -- 



  Livestock Mixed Ahead of WASDE Release -- Market Talk 



  0957 ET - Today's WASDE report from the USDA will include fresh projections for U.S. beef and pork, which will likely create movements in futures come next week. For cattle, futures are slightly higher, but prices remain roughly 8 cents a pound off from the record of $2.40 a pound set late last month. "On one hand, the futures recovery has seemingly improved overall sentiment, but on the other hand we're still watching both spot cash cattle and cutout markets trade lower this week," says StoneX in a note. Live cattle rises 0.3%, lean hogs slip 0.2%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com) 



  -- 



  U.S. Natural Gas Recovers Ground -- Market Talk 



  0954 ET - U.S. natural gas futures remain in a choppy trade around the $3 level with rally attempts held back by surplus inventories heading into the low-demand shoulder season. Yesterday's EIA report showed storage up 71 Bcf at 3,343 Bcf, or 188 Bcf above the five-year average. "A late-season hurricane or mild October pose risks for a storage overbuild and rapidly deteriorating gas prices," Eli Rubin of EBW Analytics says in a note. But "eventually, even normal October weather may allow a bullish weather-normalized supply/demand balance to rapidly winnow storage surpluses to the five-year norm." Nymex natural gas is up 2.1% at $2.995/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com) 



  -- 



  Grains Mixed Ahead of Today's WASDE Report -- Market Talk 



  0925 ET - CBOT grain futures have a mixed showing in premarket trade ahead of this afternoon's WASDE report. Analysts surveyed by WSJ this week are forecasting small decreases to the outlook for corn and soybean production and yields, but in the case of corn, output is expected to remain well above record highs. Strong figures reported by Brazilian crop agency Conab for that country's harvest are also keeping pre-WASDE trading tentative. Most-active CBOT corn is up 0.4%, soybeans fall 0.1%, and wheat drops 0.5%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com) 



  -- 



  Equinor Faces Gas-Price Drop and Lower Financial Flexibility -- Market Talk 



  1308 GMT - Equinor faces challenges from an expected decline in European gas prices and lower financial flexibility due to its participation in Orsted's rights issue, UBS analysts write. European gas storage has refilled steadily, while at the same time new LNG projects have started up. Longer term, LNG investments are picking up and UBS expects gas prices to drop even further than previously. Meanwhile, the Danish wind-farm developer has confirmed it will participate in the Orsted rights issue to maintain its 10% stake, at a cost of $939 million. "This will further increase gearing and reduce financial flexibility, increasing risks around shareholder returns into next year." UBS cuts its price target on the stock to 210 kroner ($21) from 220 kroner and reiterates its sell rating. Shares rise 0.5% to 242.50 kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com) 



  -- 



  Oil Rebounds as U.K. Targets Russia With Fresh Sanctions 



  1209 GMT - Oil prices rebound in afternoon trade after the U.K. unveiled a fresh package of 100 sanctions targeting Russia's oil flows and military machine. Brent crude rises 2% to $67.68 a barrel, while WTI is up 1.8% to $63.48 a barrel after opening the session lower on oversupply concerns. Risks of disruption to Russian energy flows continue to underpin prices, despite bearish market fundamentals, analysts say. The U.K. measures come in response to the violation of NATO airspace over Poland earlier this week and recent strikes in Kyiv. Meanwhile, the EU is currently preparing a 19th package of sanctions against Russia and reportedly considering a faster phase-out of Russian fossil fuels. Two EU officials said President Trump asked the bloc to hit some goods from China and India-two major buyers of Russian oil-with tariffs of up to 100% to ratchet up pressure on Russia. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com) 



  -- 



  Palm Oil Prices End Lower, Tracking Weakness in Soybean Oil -- Market Talk 



  1004 GMT - Palm oil prices closed lower on weaker soybean oil prices, says David Ng, a trader at Kuala Lumpur-based Iceberg X. He notes there are concerns over rising stock level in the country, pegging support for CPO futures at 4,400 ringgit a ton and resistance at 4,580 ringgit a ton. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for November delivery closed 9 ringgit lower at 4,445 ringgit a ton. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang) 



  -- 



  London's Miners Help Push FTSE 100 to Record Highs -- Market Talk 



  0843 GMT - Mining stocks have helped propel the FTSE 100 index to a record high in morning trade in London. Anglo American shares are up nearly 13% over the past 5 days on the news that it has agreed to merge with Teck Resources to create a $53 billion copper giant. Glencore is up 7%. Much of the share-price growth has been concentrated on gold and silver stocks as precious metals continue to benefit from safe-have demand. Fresnillo trades up 7%, Hochschild Mining is up 6%, with Endeavour Mining 4% higher. The FTSE 100 trades up 0.3% at 9,326 points. Over the year-to-date, it is up 14%.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com) 



  -- 



  Copper Rises as Indonesian Mine Accident Fuels Supply Concerns -- Market Talk 



  0826 GMT - Copper prices are set for strong weekly gains as operations at a large mine in Indonesia remain suspended, raising supply concerns. In early trade, three-month copper on the London Metal Exchange rises 0.5% to $10,110.50 a metric ton and is up 2.5% on the week. Phoenix-based miner Freeport-McMoRan said mining operations at the Grasberg site in Central Papua remain temporarily suspended after a mud flow incident left seven workers trapped underground. "A prolonged disruption at the second largest copper mine in the world could tighten the market," ANZ analysts say. Meanwhile, expectations of easing monetary policy in the U.S. are lifting sentiment across the industrial metals sector and brightening demand prospects. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com) 



  -- 



  Crude Extends Losses on Supply Glut Concerns, Softening U.S. Demand 



  0809 GMT - Oil prices fall in early trade, extending the previous session's losses as concerns over looming oversupply and softening U.S demand overshadow geopolitical risks in the Middle East and Eastern Europe. Brent crude slips 0.6% to $65.95 a barrel, while WTI is down 0.7% to $61.95 a barrel. The IEA now expects global supply to rise faster than previously forecast, far exceeding demand growth as OPEC+ continues to raise output. U.S. crude inventories also rose for a second straight week, underscoring softer demand in the world's largest consumer. The retreat follows days of gains fueled by prospects of stricter Western sanctions on Russian oil exports after President Trump asked the EU to hit some Chinese and Indian goods with tariffs in a new attempt to ratchet up pressure on the Kremlin. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com) 



  -- 



  European Oil Stocks Slip as Prices Remain Under Pressure -- Market Talk 



  0742 GMT - European oil stocks slip in opening trade as worries about oversupply continue to mount. Brent crude trades down 0.7% at $65.93 a barrel and prices remain under pressure. A surprise inventory build of 3.9 million barrels in the U.S. last week combined with growing concerns around oversupply as OPEC+ members plan to boost production is contributing to traders' worries, Derren Nathan, head of equity research at Hargreaves Lansdown, writes. In London, BP shares fall 0.7% and Shell drops 0.15%. France's TotalEnergies is down 0.65% while shares in Spain's Repsol and Italy's Eni are down around 0.4%. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com) 



  -- 



  (MORE TO FOLLOW) Dow Jones Newswires



  September 12, 2025 11:16 ET (15:16 GMT)

data.title: Dow Jones Top Markets Headlines at 11 AM ET: U.S. Stocks Trade Mixed | Consumer ...
data.description:
U.S. Stocks Trade Mixed

U.S. stocks were mixed after all three major indexes closed Thursday at new peaks - for the second time this week.

----

Consumer sentiment hits four-month low in September as lower- and middle-income Americans worry about jobs

Consumers were worried about rising risks to business conditions and labor markets, as well as increasing inflation.

----

Trump Pushes to Remove Fed's Lisa Cook Before Next Policy Meeting

The Federal Open Market Committee meets next week to decide on a widely expected quarter-point rate cut.

----

U.K. Targets Russia With New Sanctions on Oil Fleet, Defense Suppliers

One hundred sanctions against Russia target ships carrying Russian oil as well as companies supplying electronics, chemicals and explosives used to manufacture weapons.

----

Beijing Warns Mexico Against Tariffs That Could Harm Chinese Goods

Beijing has warned Mexico to think twice before moving ahead with a plan to raise tariffs that could hurt Chinese automakers, saying it would retaliate against the measure.

----

U.K. Economy Stagnated in July

Gross domestic product ground to a halt in July, matching consensus estimates, though the Bank of England was still expected to persist with rate cuts.

----

Russia's Central Bank Cuts Key Rate for Third Consecutive Meeting

The Bank of Russia cut its key rate to 17% from 18% as the economy and inflation slowed following two years of rapid expansion driven by government spending on the war in Ukraine.

----

Front and Center in This Week's IPOs: Individual Investors

Klarna, Gemini and others have set aside more shares for everyday buyers.

----

Stocks and bonds are sending very different messages about recession risks

The stock market keeps touching fresh record highs, while the bond market is girding for trouble in the economy.

----

Canada to Champion Mining, Energy and Port Projects in Economic Revamp

The five selected projects, which stand to help the country reduce reliance on the U.S., are expected to generate an economic boost of $43.39 billion, said Prime Minister Mark Carney.

----

Mortgage Rates Are at an 11-Month Low. Will That Save This Housing Market?

Buyers have more purchasing power with lower rates, but home prices near record highs and a weakening job market are significant hurdles. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  September 12, 2025 11:15 ET (15:15 GMT)

data.title: Dow Jones Top Company Headlines at 11 AM ET: Apple Postpones iPhone Air's Release in China | CEO ...
data.description:
Apple Postpones iPhone Air's Release in China

The company is awaiting regulatory approval for its new, thinner iPhone model in its second-largest market.

----

CEO of Tylenol Maker Lobbied RFK Jr. Not to Cite Drug as Autism Cause in Report

In a hastily scheduled meeting, Kenvue interim Chief Executive Kirk Perry sought to dissuade the health secretary, arguing there is no clear link.

----

Armani's Will Seeks Sale of Fashion Empire or Public Listing

Instructions from the late Italian designer to his heirs aim to shake up the independent label's ownership.

----

Norwegian Wealth Fund Excludes Miner Eramet From Portfolio Over Ethics Concerns

The ethics council advising the fund said there was a risk that Eramet could be contributing to environmental damage and human-rights violations.

----

RH Stock Falls as Tariff Uncertainty Hits Earnings Outlook. What to Know.

The home furnishing retailer said tariffs would add to its costs next year.

----

CVC Strikes $1.5 Billion Deal for GoDaddy Rival Namecheap

The buyout firm is poised to acquire a majority stake in the Arizona-based domain registrar and web-hosting provider.

----

Alibaba Shares Surge on Increased AI Efforts

Alibaba's shares surged as investors cheered the Chinese tech giant's recent series of moves to boost AI development and stay ahead of the race.

----

OpenAI's Funding Challenges Loom Over Oracle, Broadcom Deal Spree

The world's largest startup needs more paying users to help finance its ambitions, but researchers and consultants aren't sure they will materialize soon.

----

Paramount Skydance Prepares Ellison-Backed Bid for Warner Bros. Discovery

The bid will be for the entire company, including its cable networks and movie studio, according to people familiar with the situation.

----

A U.S. Icon Loses Out to European Rival In Rush for Air Defenses

Denmark's choice of a European system over the U.S-made Patriot comes amid friction with Trump administration and a wider European desire to wean the continent off American weaponry.

----

If Pepsi Wants to Win, It Has to Play Coke's Game

Coke shed bottling and poured money into marketing. Unless Pepsi slims down, the gap between the two will continue to widen.

----

Freight Startup Flexport Forecasts Profitability in 2025-With an Asterisk

CEO Ryan Petersen says the company is aiming for a profitable 2026.

----

Rakuten Medical to Weigh IPO to Extend Funding Beyond 2027

The president of Rakuten Medical, a developer of cancer treatments partly owned by Japan's Rakuten Group, said he would consider an initial public offering to raise funds after current funding runs out in two years.

----

Sabadell Board Recommends Shareholders Don't Accept BBVA's $18 Billion Offer

The board's opinion comes as the acceptance period for BBVA's hostile bid opened on Monday and runs until October 7.

----

Winklevoss twins get their $2 billion payday as Gemini's stock gets set to trade

IPO for the cryptocurrency exchange founded by billionaires Tyler and Cameron Winklevoss prices above expectations, in a sign of strong demand. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  September 12, 2025 11:15 ET (15:15 GMT)
